# Triggery — full LLM context

> Single-file reference bundle for LLM consumers. Combines the README pitch, architecture, API surface, packages, deployment story, performance methodology, and contribution model. Source of truth: <https://github.com/triggeryjs/triggery>. Pre-1.0; API unstable.

For the short index, see [`llms.txt`](./llms.txt).

---

## 1. What Triggery is

Triggery is a declarative, hook-first orchestration layer for React, Solid, and Vue. It expresses scenarios of the form **event → conditions → actions** as first-class objects, one file per scenario.

- Not a state manager. State lives in Zustand / Redux / Jotai / MobX / signals — Triggery only consumes it through pull-only conditions.
- Not a reactive engine. There is no incremental graph or propagation pipeline.
- Not an event emitter. Events are typed and routed via indexed dispatch.
- Not a state machine library. Use XState for formal state charts.

What it replaces in practice: prop-drilled callbacks, ad-hoc contexts wired up just to make one component call another, side-effect logic glued to UI components, and single scenarios scattered across three or four features.

---

## 2. Architecture overview

Each runtime is an isolated instance of the engine:

```
createRuntime() → Runtime {
  registry:         Map<triggerId, RuntimeTrigger>
  eventIndex:       Map<eventKey, RuntimeTrigger[]>
  scheduler:        MicrotaskScheduler (default) | SyncScheduler
  middleware:       ordered MiddlewareChain
  inspector:        ring buffer of last N runs
  conditionStores:  per-scope Map of port → getter[]
  actionStores:     per-scope Map of port → handler stack
}
```

Dispatching a single event runs the following pipeline:

```
fireEvent(eventKey, payload)
  └─ middleware.onFire?            (mutate / cancel / annotate)
  └─ scheduler.enqueue(...)        (microtask flush by default)
  └─ for each trigger in eventIndex[eventKey]:
       1. middleware.onBeforeMatch?
       2. required-gate (lazy reads)        → skip with reason if missing
       3. SnapshotProxy (memoized lazy reads)
       4. ActionsProxy (debounce/throttle/concurrency)
       5. AbortController + signal for async
       6. middleware.onActionStart/onActionEnd/onError per action
       7. inspector.record(run)
```

Invariants:

- **Snapshot consistency.** Inside one run, `conditions.x` always returns the same value.
- **No render coupling.** `fireEvent` does not trigger React/Solid/Vue re-renders by itself.
- **No global eval.** Triggers are only visited via the indexed lookup for the fired `eventKey`.

---

## 3. The trigger file

The single source of a scenario looks like this:

```ts
// triggers/message.trigger.ts
import { createTrigger } from '@triggery/core';

export const messageTrigger = createTrigger<{
  events: { 'new-message': { author: string; text: string; channelId: string } };
  conditions: {
    user: { id: string; name: string };
    settings: { sound: boolean; notifications: boolean };
    activeChannelId: string | null;
  };
  actions: { showToast: { title: string; body: string }; playSound: 'beep' | 'mod-alert' };
}>({
  id: 'message-received',
  events: ['new-message'],
  required: ['user', 'settings'],
  handler({ event, conditions, actions, check }) {
    if (!conditions.user || !conditions.settings) return;
    if (conditions.activeChannelId === event.payload.channelId) return;
    if (event.payload.author === conditions.user.name) return;
    if (!check.is('settings', (s) => s.notifications)) return;

    actions.showToast?.({ title: event.payload.author, body: event.payload.text });

    if (check.is('settings', (s) => s.sound)) {
      actions.playSound?.('beep');
    }
  },
});
```

Six files for the whole feature: one trigger + five port providers. The trigger reads like a spec.

---

## 4. Hooks API (identical across framework bindings)

### `useEvent(trigger, eventName)`

Returns a `(payload) => void` fire function. Calling it enqueues an event under the active runtime's scheduler.

### `useCondition(trigger, conditionName, getter, deps?)`

Registers a pull-only getter. The runtime calls the getter at fire time, never push-subscribes. Re-renders of the host component do not invalidate the condition unless `deps` changed.

### `useAction(trigger, actionName, handler)`

Registers an action handler. Last-mount-wins with a DEV warn-once when colliding.

### `useInlineTrigger(config)`

Inline single-file trigger for ad-hoc reactions inside a component:

```tsx
useInlineTrigger({
  on: 'analytics:button-click',
  do: (payload) => track('cta', payload),
});
```

### `useInspectHistory(trigger?)` (React/Solid/Vue)

Subscribes to the inspector ring buffer. Returns recent runs filtered to a specific trigger or to the whole runtime.

### `<TriggerRuntimeProvider runtime={runtime}>`

Wraps the subtree in an explicit runtime. Required when not using a globally-default runtime.

### `<TriggerScope id="chat">`

Scope-discriminates port registrations. Only triggers declared with the same `scope` see registrations inside.

---

## 5. Action options and concurrency

Chainable wrappers per call:

```ts
actions.throttle(5000).playSound('mod-alert');
actions.debounce(1000).showToast({ title, body });
actions.queue.fetchUser(id, { concurrency: 'queue' });
```

Concurrency strategies:

- `sync` — fire-and-forget; never aborts.
- `take-latest` *(default for async)* — abort the previous run via `AbortController.abort('superseded-by-latest')`.
- `take-every` — let all runs proceed.
- `take-first` — drop subsequent runs while one is in flight.
- `queue` — serialize runs.
- `exhaust` — alias for `take-first`.

`take-latest` aborts on HMR (`abort('hmr')`) and on runtime/scope teardown.

---

## 6. Schedulers

Built-in:

- `microtask` *(default)*. Coalesces multiple `fire`s in the same tick.
- `sync`. Immediate dispatch; useful in `runtime.fireSync(eventKey, payload)` for tests.

V1 will add `animation-frame` and `idle`. V2 — `priority` over React's scheduler.

---

## 7. Runtime options

```ts
createRuntime({
  middleware: [reduxDevtools(), sentry()],
  maxCascadeDepth: 3,
  inspectorBufferSize: 200,
  inspector: false | true | { dev?: boolean; prod?: boolean },
});
```

Inspector defaults: enabled in DEV, disabled in PROD (via `process.env.NODE_ENV` check at runtime construction). The object form lets you override one environment without changing the other. Setting `inspector: false` removes ring-buffer allocation and inspector snapshot work from the hot path.

---

## 8. Cascade safety

Cascade (an action that fires another trigger's event) is allowed with hard guarantees:

- `maxCascadeDepth` defaults to 3. Overflow → `onCascade('overflow')` → DEV throw, prod skip with the cascade tree visible in the inspector.
- Cycle detection: every top-level fire creates a `cascadeId` and a `Set<triggerId> visited`. Re-entering `visited` → `onCascade('cycle')`.
- `createRuntime({ cascade: { maxDepth, onOverflow, onCycle } })` lets you tune behaviour.

---

## 9. Ownership and scopes

Default model: **last-mount-wins** with a DEV warn-once per `(port, scope)` collision.

Opt-in alternatives:

- `useCondition('user', getUser, { strategy: 'stackable', merge })` — custom merge.
- `useCondition('user', getUser, { strategy: 'first-wins' })` — ignore later registrations.

Scope segregation via `<TriggerScope id="chat">`. Full isolation (separate inspector + scheduler) via `createRuntime()` + `<TriggerRuntimeProvider>`.

---

## 10. SSR / RSC behaviour

- On the server, `useEvent` / `useCondition` / `useAction` return no-op functions. `fire` does nothing.
- Hydration mismatch is impossible because no DOM-side state is read.
- A real server runtime (`@triggery/server`) lands in V2.

---

## 11. Lifecycle and edge cases

| Scenario | Behaviour |
|---|---|
| React StrictMode double-mount | Refcount per `(scope, instance)`. `unregister` decrements; deletes at zero. |
| Suspense fallback / Offscreen | Required condition not yet provided → handler skipped with `pending-condition`. |
| HMR | Module version stored in registry. On HMR, in-flight runs aborted with `'hmr'`. New version replaces handler. Inspector preserves history. |
| React Native | Works out of the box. No DOM adapters needed. |
| Multi-tab | Opt-in via `@triggery/broadcast` (V2). |

---

## 12. Packages

### Core

- `@triggery/core` — the runtime + `createTrigger`. Zero deps.
- `@triggery/testing` — `createTestRuntime`, `mockCondition`, `mockAction`, `flushMicrotasks`. For unit tests without rendering.
- `@triggery/vite` — Vite plugin: auto-imports every `*.trigger.ts` via a virtual module + HMR helpers.

### Framework bindings

- `@triggery/react` — React 18/19 bindings.
- `@triggery/solid` — SolidJS 1.x bindings.
- `@triggery/vue` — Vue 3 bindings.

### State adapters

All adapters expose a `use*Condition(trigger, name, store, selector?)` signature and **never subscribe the host component to updates** — they read via `getState()` / `peek()` / `.value` at fire time.

- `@triggery/zustand` — Zustand stores.
- `@triggery/redux` — Redux stores (any reducer flavour: vanilla, RTK, redux-thunk, redux-saga).
- `@triggery/jotai` — Jotai atoms.
- `@triggery/mobx` — MobX observables.
- `@triggery/reatom` — Reatom v1000+ (callable atoms).
- `@triggery/signals` — `@preact/signals-core`, `alien-signals`, any `peek()`/`.value` signal.
- `@triggery/query` — TanStack Query cache; reads `queryCache.find()` lazily.

### Event-source adapters

- `@triggery/dom` — `useDomEvent`, `useResizeObserver`, `useIntersectionObserver`.
- `@triggery/socket` — `useSocketIoEvent` (socket.io-client) and `useWebSocketEvent` (native WebSocket).

### DevTools

- `@triggery/devtools-redux` — middleware that streams runtime events into the Redux DevTools browser extension. Action names: `triggery/<trigger-id>/fire`, `triggery/<trigger-id>/action/<action-name>`.
- `@triggery/devtools-panel` — drop-in React components for in-app inspection: `<InspectorView>`, `<TriggerSnapshotView>`.
- `@triggery/devtools-bridge` — page-side bridge for external inspectors.
- `extensions/chrome-devtools` — Chrome DevTools panel over `@triggery/devtools-bridge`. Load unpacked.

---

## 13. Examples

The repo ships at least one fully runnable example:

- [`examples/vite-react-counter`](https://github.com/triggeryjs/triggery/tree/main/examples/vite-react-counter) — minimal Vite + React + Triggery scenario. Open directly in StackBlitz: <https://stackblitz.com/github/triggeryjs/triggery/tree/main/examples/vite-react-counter>.

More examples (notifications, modal stack, onboarding, analytics, ws-realtime) land as part of Stage 6 of the roadmap.

---

## 14. Performance methodology

Benchmarks live under [`benchmarks/`](https://github.com/triggeryjs/triggery/tree/main/benchmarks). Two suites:

- **`benchmarks/bench/core/`** — Triggery's own dispatch hot path. Run under CodSpeed `simulation` mode (Valgrind cycle counting) for maximum regression sensitivity. Pure JS, no system calls.
- **`benchmarks/bench/vs/`** — ten side-by-side scenarios against effector, rxjs, redux-saga, xstate, reatom, mobx. Run under CodSpeed `walltime` mode because the comparison libraries make system calls (RxJS schedulers, saga setTimeout, MobX/Reatom reactivity graphs) that instrumentation cannot consistently account for.

Local reproduction: `pnpm bench`. Live dashboard: <https://codspeed.io/triggeryjs/triggery>. Full breakdown plus per-scenario analysis: [`benchmarks/COMPARISONS.md`](https://github.com/triggeryjs/triggery/blob/main/benchmarks/COMPARISONS.md).

---

## 15. Tooling

- TypeScript strict, `noUncheckedIndexedAccess`, `verbatimModuleSyntax`, ESM-only.
- Biome for lint + format.
- Vitest 4.x for tests.
- tsup for ESM-only builds, `exports` field is the source of truth for entry points.
- Changesets for versioning + npm Trusted Publishers (OIDC) for publishing with provenance.

---

## 16. Project layout

```
triggery/
├── packages/
│   ├── core/                    # Runtime + createTrigger
│   ├── react/  solid/  vue/     # Framework bindings (same API)
│   ├── testing/                 # createTestRuntime, mockCondition, mockAction
│   ├── vite/                    # Vite plugin (auto-discovery + HMR)
│   ├── zustand/ redux/ jotai/ mobx/ reatom/ signals/ query/  # State adapters
│   ├── dom/    socket/          # Event-source adapters
│   └── devtools-redux/  devtools-bridge/  devtools-panel/
├── extensions/
│   └── chrome-devtools/         # DevTools panel extension
├── examples/
│   └── vite-react-counter/      # Minimal Vite+React example
├── benchmarks/
│   ├── bench/core/              # Triggery hot-path benches (simulation mode)
│   └── bench/vs/                # Side-by-side comparisons (walltime mode)
└── .github/workflows/           # CI, bench, release, security, contributors
```

---

## 17. Stability and roadmap

Pre-1.0. Phases planned (full text: [ROADMAP.md](https://github.com/triggeryjs/triggery/blob/main/ROADMAP.md)):

1. Foundation (done).
2. MVP core (done).
3. DX wrapping (done — concurrency, cascade, scope, testing kit).
4. Auto-discovery + DevTools (done — Vite plugin, redux-devtools, devtools-panel, chrome-extension).
5. State + event adapters (done — 9 production-ready packages).
6. Tooling (Stage 5 of roadmap; ESLint plugin, codemod, CLI).
7. Docs + canonical examples.
8. RC → 1.0.

Post-1.0: `@triggery/devtools-replay` (record/replay), `@triggery/sentry`, `@triggery/otel`, `@triggery/server` (Node/Edge runtime), opt-in reactive conditions, VSCode extension, Webpack/Rspack plugins.

---

## 18. Contribution model

Read [`CONTRIBUTING.md`](https://github.com/triggeryjs/triggery/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md). Highlights:

- Conventional Commits format. Subject ≤ 72 chars, imperative, lowercase, no period.
- One logical change per PR. `pnpm lint && pnpm typecheck && pnpm test && pnpm build` must be green before pushing.
- Changeset required for any change to `packages/*`. `pnpm changeset` walks you through it.
- RFCs for changes to public API surface / runtime semantics. Open an RFC issue and get ≥ 1 maintainer approval before writing code.
- No AI-attribution trailers in commits or PRs. Attribute to humans.

---

## 19. Reporting issues

- **Bug** → <https://github.com/triggeryjs/triggery/issues/new?template=bug_report.yml>.
- **Feature** → <https://github.com/triggeryjs/triggery/issues/new?template=feature_request.yml>.
- **RFC** → <https://github.com/triggeryjs/triggery/issues/new?template=rfc.yml>.
- **Security** → private channel via <https://github.com/triggeryjs/triggery/security/advisories/new> or email `a@skhom.ru`. See [SECURITY.md](https://github.com/triggeryjs/triggery/blob/main/SECURITY.md).
- **Question** → <https://github.com/triggeryjs/triggery/discussions/new?category=q-a>.

---
